cataloging_subject.label: Exposition of the Pater Noster
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English verse exposition of the Lord's Prayer indexed as DIMEV 1573 / IMEV 958, represented here by Frank A. Patterson's 1916 transcription of Cambridge University Library MS Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r. Patterson's article title, introductory paragraph, notes, signature, and affiliation are separately accounted source paratext, not medieval authorship, language, prose form, genre, or subject facets. Bodleian Library MS. Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r, is a collateral witness, not a source of Patterson's text or the U-M encoding.

catalog_summary.title: Exposition of the Pater Noster
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: By the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved; first known publication 1916
catalog_summary.region: England; exact composition locality unknown. DIMEV indexes the Cambridge codex to East Anglia, but that witness-level localization is not treated as a composition place

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus from DIMEV and the Medieval West Midlands catalogue

titles.value: A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer
notes: This is a modern editorial title and does not establish medieval self-identification as a sermon.

titles.value: God of heaven that sittest in throne
God of hefne, þat sittest in trone
ffor loue of his moder dere / Amen, sigge we alle yfere. Amen.

scope.description: Represented Cambridge witness, including the separately encoded terminal Amen

agents.name: Frank A. Patterson
notes: Editor, direct transcriber, and author of the 1916 article's modern paratext; not the anonymous medieval author.

date_statements.display: Before or by the represented late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century Cambridge witness; exact composition date unknown

assertion.notes: This is a terminus from the represented witness, not an equation of manuscript production with composition.

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth century according to James and Pink; Patterson allows the early fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The range preserves the inspected sources' dating difference rather than selecting one silently.

date_statements.display: 1916 (first known publication, by Frank A. Patterson)

assertion.notes: Patterson said that he believed the poem had never been published; DIMEV v1.0 lists his edition for the represented witness.

places.label: England
notes: Broad cultural and linguistic context only; no precise place of composition is established.

assertion.notes: DIMEV's East Anglia entry describes CUL Dd.11.89 at manuscript-index level; it is not promoted to a composition-place claim. The two analyzed Douce Prick of Conscience hands end before this work, and MWM says the other texts have not been linguistically analyzed.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The one encoded Latin marginal petition label and modern English article paratext are source-layer features and do not make the abstract medieval work bilingual.

form_statements.description: A Middle English poem in aa rhyming couplets. The represented Cambridge transcription has 596 L elements in five XML verse groups, followed by a separately encoded terminal Amen; Patterson's prose introduction and signature remain separate source paratext.

assertion.notes: Patterson titled the edition a sermon and describes clerical inculcation of virtue; DIMEV more neutrally calls it an exposition.

subject_statements.label: The Lord's Prayer and its seven petitions
Sin, forgiveness, temptation, and moral instruction
The seven deadly sins and Last Judgment

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English exposition of the Lord's Prayer in rhyming couplets. After presenting and interpreting the prayer's seven petitions, the poem develops instruction on divine fatherhood, Christian conduct, sin and forgiveness, temptation, the seven deadly sins, the Trinity, Christ's Passion, the Eucharist, judgment, and salvation. CME00111 represents Patterson's 1916 transcription of the Cambridge witness; his short modern introduction and signed article frame are present in the source item but are not part of the medieval work.

extent.description: Whole encoded article accounting: one DIV1 and one HEAD; one modern introductory P; five medieval LG groups containing 596 L elements; two NOTE elements; 140 MILESTONE elements; thirteen PB elements marking printed pp. 406-418; two CLOSER elements (the medieval terminal Amen and Patterson's signed closer); one SIGNED element; one LB; and 78 HI elements. Seventy-seven HI elements occur inside medieval verse lines and one marks Patterson's Columbia University affiliation. Patterson's printed marginal line number 90 occurs twice, at XML verse lines 90 and 95; later printed numbers are five lower than XML position, so printed 590 marks XML line 595 and the final verse is XML line 596.

content_parts.label: Patterson's article title and introduction
content_parts.summary: The modern article heading and one prose paragraph identifying the manuscript, proposing a late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century date, describing the poem's didactic interest, and citing the older Cambridge catalogue.

locators.value: DIV1/HEAD and direct P before LG[1]

notes: Modern English editorial paratext is not included in work-level language, prose-form, genre, subject, or authorship discovery fields.

content_parts.label: Exposition of the Pater Noster
content_parts.summary: The anonymous Middle English couplet exposition represented by the Cambridge witness, from the invocation to the communal final Amen.

locators.value: LG[1]-LG[5], 596 L elements, and first CLOSER

notes: The separate first CLOSER contains the medieval terminal Amen and belongs to the work even though it is not an L element.
The 596 count describes the represented XML/transcription, not every witness or an abstract-work critical lineation.

content_parts.label: Patterson signature and affiliation
content_parts.summary: The modern signed closer naming Frank A. Patterson and Columbia University after the poem.
notes: The italic Columbia University affiliation accounts for the one HI element outside the medieval verse.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD, IDG, TEXT/BODY/DIV1, P, LG, L, NOTE, CLOSER, and SIGNED
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifiers, encoded article boundary, modern paratext, Middle English poem, subjects, printed pagination and lineation, manuscript folio milestones, notes, closing Amen, signature, and italic typography.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XML structure and lineation audit of CME00111.xml.
evidence.locator: Element counts, nesting, printed page groups, folio milestones, and numbered-line positions
evidence.summary: Confirms 31,950 bytes; one DIV1; one HEAD; one P; five LG; 596 L; two NOTE; 140 MILESTONE; thirteen PB; two CLOSER; one SIGNED; one LB; and 78 HI elements, of which 77 occur inside verse lines. It also confirms the duplicated printed milestone 90 and the resulting five-line offset through the end.

evidence.citation: Frank A. Patterson, 'A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer,' The Journal of English and Germanic Philology 15 (1916): 406-418.
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for article title and authorship, direct Cambridge shelfmark and folio milestones, Patterson's dating and publication claim, source-catalog reference, full transcription, notes, typography, duplicated line number 90, terminal Amen, and signed closer.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, record 1573, IMEV/NIMEV 958.
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-1573; MSSIndex.xml CULDd1189 and BodDou141; Bibliography.xml Patterson1916b
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence for normalized incipit, exposition title, Lord's Prayer subject, two-line aa form, IMEV/NIMEV identifier, Cambridge and Douce witnesses and loci, Patterson edition, and manuscript-index-level East Anglia localization of CUL Dd.11.89.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, unpublished descriptions by H. L. Pink and M. R. James of MS Dd.11.89, published as Apollo datasets in 2021.
evidence.locator: whole-codex description; target begins f. 186 and ends f. 196a; f. 196v blank
evidence.summary: Official repository evidence for present shelfmark, late-fourteenth-century dating in James and Pink, material, dimensions, leaves, layout, several hands, and the target's exact f. 186r-196r boundary and explicit. These are historical unpublished descriptions, not a silently substituted current full manuscript catalogue.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of Vernacular Manuscript Books of the English West Midlands, Bodleian Library MS. Douce 141 entry.
evidence.summary: Scholarly manuscript-catalog evidence for the collateral Douce witness's uniform title, IMEV 958 identity, exact ff. 130r-138r boundary, incipit and explicit, codex date and physical description, and the explicit warning that texts after the analyzed Prick of Conscience hands have not been linguistically analyzed.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Douce 141.
evidence.locator: whole codex; current shelfmark, summary contents, physical description, history, and partial surrogate
evidence.summary: Current official summary evidence for MS. Douce 141 / former SC 21715, first-half-fifteenth-century English parchment codex, physical extent, ownership, and the existence of only a two-image Digital Bodleian object in the public catalog.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00111.
evidence.summary: Linked graph evidence for the repository-to-U-M-to-Patterson-to-Cambridge path; collateral Douce witness; complete cached edition, catalogue, and data objects; partial Bodleian slides; current reproduction routes; scoped rights; and unresolved facsimile and editorial questions.

open_questions.question: When and where was the poem composed, and what work-specific dialect evidence can be established for each surviving witness?
next_steps: Consult modern work-specific scholarship beyond the two-witness DIMEV record and historical descriptions.
Distinguish composition language from each target hand before adding a narrower region facet.
Do not transfer DIMEV's codex-level East Anglia label or the analyzed Douce ff. 1r-129v hands to this work without target-specific evidence.

open_questions.question: What exact manuscript features do Patterson's 77 italic spans inside the poem represent?
next_steps: Obtain complete Cambridge page images and compare every italic span against ff. 186r-196r.
Separate abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, uncertain readings, and any other functions at segment level.
Preserve the edition's italic signal until Patterson's undocumented convention can be verified directly.

open_questions.question: Can complete, reusable color facsimiles of CUL Dd.11.89 and Bodleian MS. Douce 141 be obtained and mapped at work level?
next_steps: Ask Cambridge Imaging Services for a whole-codex quote and exact reuse terms, then map ff. 186r-196r within the supplied object.
Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether complete photography or an existing microfilm/digital surrogate can be supplied, then map ff. 130r-138r.
Record any supplied whole-object files in the ignored source cache without inferring public redistribution rights from private research access.
notes: No complete public copy was found in the dated search scope; this does not establish that either manuscript has never been digitized.
The work is anonymous; Patterson is editor and transcriber, not author.
Patterson's a/b folio notation maps to modern recto/verso: f. 186a-f. 196a is recorded as ff. 186r-196r. James explicitly says the poem ends on 196a and 196v is blank.
The two surviving witnesses identified by DIMEV are Cambridge Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r, and Bodleian Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r. Patterson used the Cambridge witness only; Douce is collateral.

Printed marginal line number 90 is duplicated. Consequently the XML's actual 596 verse lines must not be reported as Patterson line 596: his last displayed number is 590 beside XML line 595, followed by one unnumbered verse line and the terminal Amen closer.
The one Latin marginal petition label and Patterson's modern English frame are retained as source-layer details but are excluded from abstract-work language and prose-form discovery.

The complete JEGP volume, OCR, Internet Archive metadata, DIMEV v1.0 release, both Cambridge description datasets, the complete MWM data release, the current Bodleian catalogue TEI, and the complete published two-slide Bodleian object are cached under source-cache/CME00111. The cache remains Git-ignored.
